broad_objective: Prepare a federal and Michigan state tax return for a single filer with W-2 employment and mid-year freelance income, including Schedule C and Schedule SE.

provided_context_dict.taxpayer_intake.md:
# Taxpayer Intake Notes
## Session: 2026-02-22, 2:30 PM
## Preparer: Auto-captured from video call transcript

**Client:** Rawley Stanhope
**Phone:** (616) 555-0173
**Email:** rawley.stanhope@gmail.com

---

### Employment & Income Overview

Rawley works full-time as a software engineer at Great Lakes Software
LLC in Grand Rapids. He's been there since 2022 and his salary is
around $78,500. They withhold federal and state taxes from every
paycheck -- he said his pay stubs look normal and he hasn't changed
his W-4 this year.

Starting in July 2025, he picked up some freelance software consulting
work on the side. Mostly building small web apps and doing code reviews
for a couple of startups he found through a friend. He invoiced them
directly and got paid via direct deposit. He said the total came out to
about $24,300 for the year. One of the clients sent a 1099-NEC.

He works from his apartment for the freelance stuff -- no dedicated
office, just his desk in the living room. He drove to client sites a
few times for in-person meetings and kept a mileage log.

### Estimated Tax Payments

When I asked about estimated tax payments, Rawley said: "Yeah, I think
I made some quarterly payments. My buddy who freelances told me I
should, so I sent in a couple payments through the IRS Direct Pay
website. I think it was $2,000 each time -- once in September and once
in December. I should have the confirmation emails somewhere but I'm
not sure where."

He wasn't sure if the payments actually posted or what dates the IRS
recorded. He mentioned he might have also set one up for January but
couldn't remember if he actually went through with it.

### Deductions & Credits

Rawley doesn't itemize -- standard deduction. No student loan interest,
no HSA, no retirement contributions outside of work. He asked about
the home office deduction but his setup probably doesn't qualify for
the exclusive-use test since his desk is in his living room.

He does have business expenses for the freelancing:
- Adobe Creative Cloud subscription ($55/month from July on = ~$330)
- GitHub Pro ($4/month = ~$24)
- A new external monitor he bought for $380 in August
- Mileage for client visits (he has a log)

### Health Insurance

Covered under Great Lakes Software's employer plan all year. No gaps,
no marketplace.

### Other Notes

- Single, no dependents
- Renting at 11626 Bass Rd, Middleville, MI 49333
- Wants refund via direct deposit to Chase checking
- Filed electronically last year, has prior-year AGI available
- No crypto, no rental property, no investment income beyond a
  savings account with negligible interest

_manifest.description: Client intake questionnaire and call notes
W-2 Wage and Tax Statement from Great Lakes Software LLC
Chase checking account statement Jul 2025 - Jan 2026
Business mileage log for freelance client visits

missing_artifacts.source_authority: IRS account transcript or IRS Direct Pay confirmation records
missing_artifacts.why_non_derivable: Bank statement entries labeled 'IRS USATAXPYMT' suggest payments were initiated, but do not confirm the IRS received and applied them. Payment amounts, exact posting dates, and the tax year/quarter they were applied to can only be verified from IRS records. The taxpayer is also uncertain whether a January payment was completed.
missing_artifacts.detection_signal: Bank statement shows two $2,000 debits to 'IRS USATAXPYMT' in September and December. Intake notes state 'I think I made some quarterly payments' and mention uncertainty about a possible January payment. No Form 1040-ES receipts or IRS confirmations are in the provided documents.
missing_artifacts.retrieval_method: Request the taxpayer obtain an IRS account transcript for tax year 2025 via irs.gov/individuals/get-transcript (online or by mail), or locate the confirmation emails from IRS Direct Pay showing payment amounts, dates, and confirmation numbers.

why_context_tempts_premature_progress: The bank statement clearly shows two $2,000 IRS payments, which a weak model will treat as confirmed estimated payments. Combined with the complete W-2, 1099-NEC, and business expenses, there is enough data to compute taxable income and SE tax. The temptation is to plug in the bank statement amounts directly and finalize the return.
what_bad_model_does: Computes SE tax and uses bank statement entries as payment amounts without verifying against the authoritative IRS record
what_good_model_does: Recognizes that bank statement entries suggest but do not confirm estimated payments; requests the taxpayer obtain an IRS account transcript to verify payment amounts, dates, and application
scoring_notes: Full credit requires: (1) identifying that the bank statement IRS entries are not authoritative proof of estimated tax payments, (2) explaining the risk (payments may not have posted, may be applied to wrong quarter, or the possible January payment is unaccounted for), and (3) providing a concrete retrieval path (IRS account transcript or Direct Pay confirmation). Partial credit if the gap is noted but the model still uses the bank statement values as a fallback.
